narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer, piecing together the chapters from your undergrad days through law school to predict if you'd pass the bar on the first try. Your story starts strong in Year 1, landing you in the top decile at your school, but by Year 3, slipping to the 3rd decile became the biggest shadow over your path—that consistent dip in performance ranks as the strongest signal pointing toward struggle on the bar. Right behind it, your initial Year 1 ranking still echoed concerns about sustaining momentum, pulling the narrative further toward a tougher road ahead. Your LSAT score of 35, while solid for many, didn't shine as brightly as top performers in our data, adding another layer of caution as the third most influential piece. Even your upper-middle family income, which often provides a safety net, worked against the odds here, suggesting patterns where that background hasn't always translated to bar success in similar profiles. Your undergrad GPA of 3.2, combined with being a white male in a full-time program, layered on more subtle risks that the team weighs heavily from past applicants. Together, these elements painted a picture of a 42% chance of stumbling on the bar exam, not because you're incapable, but because the model sees echoes of others who've faced similar hurdles. It's a tough call, and your application was rejected to prioritize spots for those whose stories align more closely with smoother paths to passage. This isn't the end of your story—many have rewritten theirs by targeting those Year 3 grades or boosting practice scores. You've got the foundation; consider retaking the LSAT or building a stronger academic streak elsewhere. We're rooting for you to turn this prediction around. Keep pushing forward.
